compiled_state.title: As a developer, I can define a recipe in the registry and execute it via CLI to get structured JSON output
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
## Description

**What**: Recipe registry schema (recipe-registry.yaml) with JSON schema validation, recipe-executor.sh execution engine, and the add-parameter recipe for Python via Rope — the minimum end-to-end walking skeleton.
**Why**: Proves the core concept: define a recipe declaratively, execute it deterministically, get structured results. All subsequent recipes build on this foundation.
**Scope**:
- IN: recipe-registry.yaml schema design (accommodating both transform and generative recipe types), JSON schema validation at load time, recipe-executor.sh (engine resolution, version validation, timeout, git stash rollback for both file modification and file creation, structured JSON output with files_changed/transforms_applied/errors/exit_code), env var parameterization for recipe parameters, degraded status for missing engines, add-parameter recipe for Python via Rope, engine adapter interface specification
- OUT: TypeScript recipes (S2), scaffold-route (S3), normalize-imports (S4), integration tests against real codebases (S5), Ruby/Synvert recipes (deferred — registry schema supports Synvert but no recipe ships this epic)

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, a developer can add a recipe entry to recipe-registry.yaml and the registry loads with JSON schema validation catching malformed entries
  ← Satisfies: "recipe-registry.yaml maps recipe names to language, engine, CLI command template, capability description with scope, minimum engine version, and installation instructions"
- When this story is complete, a developer can run recipe-executor.sh with a recipe name and parameters, and it resolves the engine, validates version, executes with timeout, and returns structured JSON (files_changed, transforms_applied, errors, exit_code)
  ← Satisfies: "recipe-executor.sh accepts a recipe name + parameters, resolves engine, validates presence + version, executes with timeout and git stash rollback on failure, returns structured JSON"
- When this story is complete, recipe parameters are passed via environment variables or argument arrays, and a test with shell metacharacters in parameters confirms no injection
  ← Satisfies: "Recipe parameters passed via env vars or argument arrays, never shell string interpolation"
- When this story is complete, running the executor with a missing engine returns a structured degraded status JSON with the engine name
  ← Satisfies: "Missing engine returns structured degraded status"
- When this story is complete, add-parameter for Python updates a function signature and all callers in a multi-file Python project via Rope
  ← Satisfies: "Initial catalog includes add-parameter (cross-file signature + caller update)"
- When this story is complete, running add-parameter twice on the same input produces identical output, and 3 consecutive runs produce zero-variance output (hash comparison)
  ← Satisfies: "Each recipe is idempotent" and "Determinism: 3 consecutive runs produce zero-variance output"
- When this story is complete, the engine adapter interface is documented (input format, output format, error codes, working directory conventions, invocation pattern) so that S2-S4 implement against a defined contract
  ← Satisfies: cross-story contract for parallel recipe implementation
- Unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Security] Parameters through shell — env var parameterization must prevent injection even with adversarial input
- [Reliability] Git stash rollback must handle both dirty worktree (unstaged changes present) and file creation (not just modification) — rollback of a generative recipe requires file deletion, not stash pop
- [Reliability] Timeout portability (GNU vs BSD timeout) — use portable timeout mechanism
- [Maintainability] Registry schema must accommodate both transform recipes (modify existing files) and generative recipes (create new files with output path, template params) — S3 should not need to modify the schema

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
